answers.question: List of scenarios
answer: Hot House World (NGFS Current Policies) [SSP3 - RCP 7.0]
answers.evidence: This led to the adoption of the Orderly (NGFS Net-Zero 2050), Disorderly (NGFS Delayed Transition) and Hot House World (NGFS Current Policies) scenarios. ... Inclusion of a balanced spread of the scenarios: low warming ([SSP1- RCP 1.9]), medium warming ( [SSP2 – RCP 4.5]), and high warming ([SSP3 – RCP 7.0]) scenarios.
answers.location_hint: Page 5 of 12, Climate scenario analysis section

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: Orderly scenario – likely warming 1.4°C (ranging from 1.0 to 1.8°C); Disorderly scenario – likely warming 2.7°C (ranging from 2.1 to 3.5°C); Hot House World scenario – likely warming 3.6°C (ranging from 2.8 to 4.6°C). The document also references a requirement for a 1.5°C scenario and a 3°C or greater scenario under NZ CS 1.
answers.evidence: Orderly - likely warming 1.4°C (ranging from 1.0 to 1.8°C) [SSP1 - RCP 1.9] | Disorderly - likely warming 2.7°C (ranging from 2.1 to 3.5°C) [SSP2 - RCP 4.5] | Hot House World - likely warming 3.6°C (ranging from 2.8 to 4.6°C) [SSP3 - RCP 7.0]
answers.notes: Temperature figures are not explicitly stated as being by 2100, but these are standard IPCC/NGFS scenario temperature outcomes which are typically referenced to 2100. No overshoot language is used.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Yes. Two providers are explicitly named: NGFS (Network for Greening the Financial System) and IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change).
answers.evidence: In FY24 Hollard commissioned an external climate advisory team to facilitate more than 10 climate workshops with the Climate-related Disclosure Working Group (CRDWG) to test our exposure and vulnerability to climate-related physical and transition risks by adopting three climate scenarios (see top row headings in Table 2. scenario analysis) from internationally recognised sources, such as the IPCC and the Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS).
answers.notes: The NGFS scenarios (Net-Zero 2050, Delayed Transition, Current Policies) are explicitly named as the adopted scenarios. IPCC is also cited as a source.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: Inclusion of a balanced spread of the scenarios: low warming ([SSP1- RCP 1.9]), medium warming ( [SSP2 – RCP 4.5]), and high warming ([SSP3 – RCP 7.0]) scenarios.
answers.notes: The document explicitly pairs SSP identifiers with RCP identifiers (SSP1-RCP1.9, SSP2-RCP4.5, SSP3-RCP7.0). These pairings are not from the same internally consistent CMIP6 scenario family — SSP1-RCP1.9 is a standard pairing, but SSP2-RCP4.5 and SSP3-RCP7.0 are non-standard cross-family combinations (SSP3 is typically paired with RCP7.0 in some frameworks, but SSP2 with RCP4.5 is also used). The document lists both SSP and RCP identifiers explicitly.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: In FY25, Hollard reassessed the climate scenarios developed in FY24 and reaffirmed their relevancy and applicability for this reporting period. ... This led to the adoption of the Orderly (NGFS Net-Zero 2050), Disorderly (NGFS Delayed Transition) and Hot House World (NGFS Current Policies) scenarios.
answers.location_hint: Page 4–5 of 12, Climate scenario analysis section
answers.notes: The document does not use language such as "downscaled", "adapted", "localised", "customised", or "tailored". The NGFS scenarios appear to be used off-the-shelf.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: In FY24 Hollard commissioned an external climate advisory team to facilitate more than 10 climate workshops with the Climate-related Disclosure Working Group (CRDWG) to test our exposure and vulnerability to climate-related physical and transition risks by adopting three climate scenarios from internationally recognised sources, such as the IPCC and the Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS).
answers.notes: While workshops were held with an internal working group and external advisors, these were to test exposure using existing NGFS/IPCC scenarios — not to co-create or co-design new scenarios. No language such as "participatory", "co-creat", "deliberat", "co-design", or "collaborative development" of scenarios is present.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.evidence: Outlined below are the scenarios that were examined. [Table 2 lists three scenarios: Orderly, Disorderly, Hot House World]
answers.location_hint: Page 4 of 12, Climate scenario analysis section
answers.notes: The document presents three climate scenarios but does not describe a separate baseline or reference scenario against which the others are compared. The "Hot House World (NGFS Current Policies)" scenario could be interpreted as a de facto baseline, but it is not explicitly described as such.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: This report contains forward looking statements including climate scenario analysis, estimations and forecasts of physical and transition risks across multiple time horizons based on climate hazard projections for HNZ using a range of projections from multiple models.
answers.notes: The document references "multiple models" but does not name any specific climate models, IAMs, or macroeconomic models. The natural peril model is mentioned generically but not named.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Capital modelling and management: Hollard conducts quarterly stress testing to assess the impact of catastrophe risk (a type of acute physical climate risk) on capital, in line with the ICAAP. The stress scenarios considered include a single extreme event as well as multiple large events; ... Claims from catastrophe events are considered a proxy for the financial impacts of climate physical-risk.
answers.location_hint: Page 10 of 12, Climate-related risk management processes, tools and methods; and Page 6 of 12, footnote 5
answers.notes: The document explicitly includes damage/loss estimates from extreme events (acute physical risks) through catastrophe modelling, stress testing, and claims from catastrophe events as a financial impact proxy.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: They can be acute, such as extreme rain and floods, cyclones, storms, storm surges, and bushfires, or chronic, such as sea level rise. ... These risks can be event-driven (acute) such as increased severity of extreme weather events. They can also be chronic, such as those related to longer-term shifts in precipitation and temperature and sea level rise. ... large destruction to physical properties, assets and Gross Domestic Product (GDP).
answers.location_hint: Page 2 of 12 (physical risks section); Page 11 of 12 (Appendix A definitions); Page 4–5 of 12 (scenario analysis table)
answers.notes: These variables are mentioned in the context of physical risks and scenario outcomes, but the document does not explicitly describe them as "drivers" of the scenarios in a modelling sense. GDP is mentioned as an impact outcome rather than a scenario input variable.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: adopting three climate scenarios (see top row headings in Table 2. scenario analysis) from internationally recognised sources, such as the IPCC and the Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS). ... This report contains forward looking statements including climate scenario analysis, estimations and forecasts of physical and transition risks across multiple time horizons based on climate hazard projections for HNZ using a range of projections from multiple models.
answers.location_hint: Page 4 of 12, footnote 1; Page 1 of 12, Disclaimer
answers.notes: The scenarios are sourced from NGFS and IPCC, which are global frameworks. There is no mention of downscaling to regional or local level. The document does not explicitly state "global" but the use of NGFS/IPCC scenarios without any downscaling language implies global scale.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short-term: Present day – 2030; Medium-term: 2030–2040; Long-term: 2040–2050. Rationale is provided: time horizons were selected based on the tenure of HNZ's existing partnerships, its strategic planning timeframe, and assessing meaningful timeframes where there has been material development in the climate scenarios to compare across the scenarios. Time horizons are defined as 20-year averages around the nominated year (2030 = average of 2020–2039; 2050 = average of 2040–2059).
answers.evidence: HNZ continued to select 2030, 2040 and 2050 as the time horizons for climate scenario analysis, based on the tenure of HNZ's existing partnerships, its strategic planning timeframe and assessing meaningful timeframes where there has been material development in the climate scenarios to compare across the scenarios. ... The time horizons are 20-year averages around the nominated year, that is, 2030 is the average of 2020 to 2039, 2050 is the average of 2040 to 2059.
answers.location_hint: Page 4 of 12, Climate scenario analysis section; footnote 2
answers.notes: Rationale is explicitly provided for the choice of time horizons.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: When determining the global warming scenarios, HNZ considered: • Industry specificity: ensuring our climate scenario analysis is aligned to industry bodies and to be easily compared with peers. This led to the adoption of the Orderly (NGFS Net-Zero 2050), Disorderly (NGFS Delayed Transition) and Hot House World (NGFS Current Policies) scenarios. • The requirements of NZ CS 1: NZ CS 1 requires the adoption of a minimum of three warming scenarios, including a 1.5 degrees Celsius climate-related scenario, a three degrees Celsius or greater climate-related scenario, and a third climate-related scenario. • Inclusion of a balanced spread of the scenarios: low warming ([SSP1- RCP 1.9]), medium warming ( [SSP2 – RCP 4.5]), and high warming ([SSP3 – RCP 7.0]) scenarios.
answers.notes: Three explicit justifications are provided: (1) industry alignment and peer comparability, (2) regulatory requirements under NZ CS 1, and (3) inclusion of a balanced spread of warming levels.
